name: Dene

nations.name: Tlicho (Dogrib)
nations.territory: Northwest Territories, north of Yellowknife to the Mackenzie valley
nations.note: Strong contemporary governance; signed the Tlicho Agreement (2003)

nations.name: Chipewyan (Dene Suline)
nations.territory: Manitoba, Saskatchewan, Alberta, NWT — the largest geographic range of any Dene nation
nations.note: Traditional caribou hunters of the barren lands transition zone

nations.name: Sahtu Dene (Bearlake, Mountain, Hare, Slavey)
nations.territory: Great Bear Lake and Mackenzie River region, Northwest Territories

nations.name: Gwich'in
nations.territory: Mackenzie Delta, Yukon, Alaska — caribou peoples of the far northwest

language: Multiple related Athabaskan languages (Chipewyan, Tlicho, Slavey, Gwich'in, etc.) — collectively 'Dene languages'
traditionalTerritories: Dene territory spans a vast area across the Northwest Territories, northern Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Alberta, and Yukon, and into Alaska. The 'Dene Nation' represents Dene peoples in the Northwest Territories specifically. Traditionally, Dene peoples were organized around seasonal movement following barren-ground caribou herds and the resources of boreal and subarctic environments.
culturalNotes: Dene means 'the people' and is the self-identifying term for Athabaskan-speaking peoples of northern Canada and Alaska. The Dene Nation (NWT) is a political organization but 'Dene' encompasses many distinct nations with their own governance and cultural practices. Dene culture is strongly oriented around the caribou — the Chipewyan in particular made extensive journeys onto the Barren Grounds to hunt barren-ground caribou. Dene oral tradition includes the Yamoria stories (the transformer/trickster figure who tied beavers to mountains across the north) and strong oral histories of specific landscape features.
heritageSkillRelevance: Caribou hide preparation — sophisticated techniques for making exceptionally warm, lightweight clothing
Moose hide tanning and clothing construction
Dene beadwork — distinctive floral bead and dentalium shell work on hide clothing
Toboggan construction — traditional sleds for subarctic winter travel
Snowshoe construction designed for the transition zone between boreal and barren lands
Fish preparation — Arctic grayling, whitefish, lake trout smoking and drying
Tanning rawhide for snowshoe webbing and lashing
Birchbark canoe construction (regional variants for subarctic rivers)
communityAuthorizedResources: NWT Archives — nwtarchives.ca — Dene historical documentation
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Caribou and moose hide preparation, beadwork, snowshoe construction, and toboggan building are Level 1 with specific Dene nation attribution (use the specific nation name when known, not just 'Dene'). Drumming and traditional ceremony protocols are Level 3-4. Medicine knowledge is Level 3.
